clean_story:
Skyscrapers as windbreakers. Streets as funnels.

The term urban wind describes how cities reshape air movement—like a river splitting around boulders, but with concrete and steel.

Buildings don’t just block wind; they redirect it. A lone tower creates a wake, slowing air behind it like a boat in water. Cluster towers together, and wind accelerates between them, squeezing through gaps like toothpaste from a tube. Chicago’s “Wind Tunnel Effect” near the Willis Tower forces pedestrians to brace against 60 mph gusts—twice the speed of open-field breezes. Hong Kong’s tightly packed skyscrapers generate downdrafts so strong they’ve toppled construction cranes.

Cities also generate wind. Asphalt and glass absorb heat, creating warm air pockets that rise, pulling cooler air inward—a phenomenon called the urban heat island effect. At night, this can spawn sudden 10 mph breezes where none existed. Rooftop gardens and reflective pavements (like Los Angeles’ “cool streets”) cut this by 3–5°C, calming the artificial winds.

Three related subjects are heat islands in deserts, bridge aerodynamics failures, and how forests soften storms.
